calendar.matters:
Notice Of Hearing On The Realreal, Inc.'S Petition To Confirm Contractual Arbitration Award
Off Calendar Oct-04-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

RESPONDENT THE REALREAL, INC.'s Petition To Confirm Contractual Arbitration Award is granted in part, and Plaintiff Taska's request to vacate or correct the award is granted. The Court strikes the award of costs and fees to Defendant from the June 29, 2020 Corrected Final Award, and confirms the award in Defendant's favor as corrected.

The Court rejects Defendant's contention that Plaintiff's challenge is untimely. Under California law, a "response requesting that an award be vacated or that an award be corrected shall be served and filed not later than 100 days after the date of service of a signed copy of the award upon: (a) The respondent if he was a party to the arbitration; or (b) The respondent's representative if the respondent was not a party to the arbitration." (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 1288.2.) However, Emergency Rule of Court 9(b), which was adopted by the California Supreme Court at the outset of the COVID-19 pandemic, provides that "[n]otwithstanding any other law, the statutes of limitations and repose for civil causes of action that are 180 days or less are tolled from April 6, 2020, until August 3, 2020." It follows that the 100-day jurisdictional period did not begin running until August 3, 2020, and did not expire until November 11, 2020.

Here, the arbitrator served the final corrected award on Plaintiff Taska on June 29, 2020. (Loisel Decl. Ex. B.) Plaintiff Taska first filed a petition to vacate the arbitrator's award on October 6, 2020, but at that time did not serve defendant "in the manner provided by law for the service of summons in an action." (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 1290.4(b).) However, the Court's docket reflects that Plaintiff properly served Defendant through its agent for service of process on October 21, 2020, before the 100-day period as tolled expired. Plaintiff's petition (on which she inexplicably did not notice a hearing until September 3, 2021) therefore was timely filed and served.

Plaintiff's argument that the arbitrator exceeded her authority is well-taken. Code of Civil Procedure section 1284 limits an arbitrator's power to correct an award to the grounds set forth in section 1286.6, subdivisions (a) and (c) (e.g., where there is "an evident miscalculation of figures" or the award is "imperfect in a matter of form, not affecting the merits of the controversy." In general, arbitrators have the power to amend their decisions to add cost and fee awards. (Evans v. Centerstone Development Co. (2005) 134 Cal.App.4th 151, 159-160; Brityz, Inc. v. Alfa-Laval Food & Dairy Co. (1995) 34 Cal.App.4th 1085, 1105-1106.) However, once an arbitrator issues a final award, "that is, an award that resolves all the issues submitted to the arbitrator," Code of Civil Procedure section 1284 "narrowly circumscribes the arbitrator's power to correct the stated resolution of those issues." (Cooper v. Lavely & Singer Professional Corp. (2014) 230 Cal.App.4th 1, 12.) In particular, "Section 1284 prohibits substantive amendments to final awards to include new awards of attorney fees." (Id. at 14.) The JAMS Rules do not alter that conclusion. (Id. at 17.) Cooper, which held that an arbitrator exceeded his powers by revising a final award denying attorney fees to include an attorney fee award, is controlling here.

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)
